headline: B40 Household Savings Rate Turns Negative at -2.8%
context: Bank Negara's 2025 Financial Stability Report revealed that B40 households have a negative savings rate of -2.8%, meaning they spend more than they earn each month, funding the gap through debt, EPF withdrawals, and informal borrowing.

cards.big: Negative savings of -2.8% means B40 families go RM120 further into debt every single month just to survive
cards.sub: 3.1 million households spending more than they earn is not poor financial planning — it's mathematically impossible wages

cards.big: B40 household average income of RM4,200 against essential expenses of RM4,320 leaves RM120 monthly deficit before any savings
cards.sub: Deficit funded by: credit cards (38%), family loans (28%), EPF withdrawal (22%), and Ah Long/Koperasi (12%)

cards.big: Financial stress linked to 42% higher incidence of anxiety and depression among B40 households per MOH 2025 data
cards.sub: Children in negative-savings households show 18% lower school attendance — economic stress becomes educational disadvantage

cards.big: Bank Negara's financial literacy programmes reach 480,000 people annually but cannot teach savings to families with negative cash flow
cards.sub: Structural solution requires wage adequacy — not financial education — but no agency has mandate to address income-expense gap

cards.big: Teaching budgeting to families who earn less than they need to survive isn't financial literacy — it's telling drowning people to swim better
Should Malaysia establish a minimum income guarantee of RM5,000 for all working households combining wages and targeted transfers?
